title: Error while writing manifest image to destination
body:
When trying to copy an image from a docker registry on an openshift cluster to another docker-registry on another openshift cluster I get the following error.

I get the same error by using different versions of skopeo, both built by me, using the official rpm on rhel and also using the `rhel-tools` image.

```
skopeo copy --src-tls-verify=false --src-creds any:**** --dest-tls-verify=false --dest-creds any:**** docker://docker-registry-default.cloudapps.someopenshiftcluster/someorg/someimage:v1.1.0-rc1 docker://docker-registry-default.cloudapps.someotherropenshiftcluster/someorg/someimage:prod
Getting image source signatures
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:b25889d273102779a1d372b4e6590da9916fe594300bf1573cb9a6521a57c25e
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:9161239f9b2210ead4fc1d528eb2167593dfe38c137c331e3dd19af0fc8cff39
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:4c26e4312208e0356276c108178b55663c8a38fc1d17e90bcf7e5a18720e5412

Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:78a76af193ea1ba85efe949c623d039cd8826eb490601da6bc576d749f08d323
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:8ccced0f514a9ba05360dddc9fb5cfdd89e78f6d5ba3a0a9b7aa6a59c43ee0ec
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:a21ad575749b51a87b4eccdc7a04a8a0084f7bf163d33902f5f7fdabdf87b3b3
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:9288470e38de40b25b12e0df23188a3f7c5056d88523d388dd3c68c574b9ba53
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:683ba06a68e23037b9d7f8ccb0b3c549aeeaacbc89b95402907cf494dd4a30cf
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:065359ed5ca2e7fda126d07cf4cd9f574462b982838a509385dbae0a38a2d132

Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:d1b259a4ee48604ec87cc764935d6f9df44816ec53a4d6c15151cadded323686
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:a3ed95caeb02ffe68cdd9fd84406680ae93d633cb16422d00e8a7c22955b46d4
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:a3ed95caeb02ffe68cdd9fd84406680ae93d633cb16422d00e8a7c22955b46d4
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:56f97170027f7e2a5631e45fcc00559c2af5456ebfaa917427fd5ecdd36bafcb
Writing manifest to image destination
time="2017-08-28T11:31:26+02:00" level=fatal msg="Error writing manifest: Error uploading manifest to someorg/someimage/manifests/prod, status 500"
```

I even tried using `atomic:` for dest but same thing.

At this point I suspect that could be something related to the fact that the two OpenShift clusters are at different versions (and probably also the inner registries, I have to verify this). 


However I opened this issue to report that this is happening and also if it's related to different versions I think that skopeo should still work IMHO.

Anyway the 5xx errors are indicative that something is wrong with the server so there's a possibility that skopeo is just showing a problem that's happening at infrastructure level.

Pushing and pulling images with the docker client (the one in RHEL) works.

Setting as destination the same as source by just changing the tag WORKS.

Using skopeo to copy the image locally and then take that image and copy to the remote DOES NOT WORK.


Wdyt?

comments.body: Please run with --debug also and paste output, thanks

Ok that command is showing that the actual problem is that the remote repo doesn't even exists (log below)

So one might ask: Worth checking if the remote repo exists before than trying to do that?

I don't think that this can be done since "non-atomic" registries like the default one in `docker/distribution` actually create the repository when you push it.

The strange thing is that it pushes the whole thing and just fail in writing the manifest while would probably be a more user friendly behavior to just deny the push like how it happens in docker hub:

```
$  docker push docker.io/fntlnz/cantpushere
The push refers to a repository [docker.io/fntlnz/cantpushere]
e58d31f33474: Preparing 
denied: requested access to the resource is denied

```

```
time="2017-08-28T14:33:13+02:00" level=debug msg="Error body {\"errors\":[{\"code\":\"UNKNOWN\",\"message\":\"unknown error\",\"detail\":{\"ErrStatus\":{\"kind\":\"Status\",\"apiVersion\":\"v1\",\"metadata\":{},\"status\":\"Failure\",\"message\":\"imagestreams \\\"myrepoe\\\" not found\",\"reason\":\"NotFound\",\"details\":{\"name\":\"myrepoe\",\"kind\":\"imagestreams\"},\"code\":404}}}]}\n" 
time="2017-08-28T14:33:13+02:00" level=debug msg="Error uploading manifest, status 500, &http.Response{Status:\"500 Internal Server Error\", StatusCode:500, Proto:\"HTTP/1.1\", ProtoMajor:1, ProtoMinor:1, Header:http.Header{\"Content-Type\":[]string{\"application/json; charset=utf-8\"}, \"Docker-Distribution-Api-Version\":[]string{\"registry/2.0\"}, \"Date\":[]string{\"Mon, 28 Aug 2017 12:33:13 GMT\"}, \"Content-Length\":[]string{\"309\"}}, Body:(*http.bodyEOFSignal)(0xc4201d5700), ContentLength:309, TransferEncoding:[]string(nil), Close:true, Uncompressed:false, Trailer:http.Header(nil), Request:(*http.Request)(0xc420276e10), TLS:(*tls.ConnectionState)(0xc420223760)}" 
time="2017-08-28T14:33:13+02:00" level=fatal msg="Error writing manifest: Error uploading manifest to myorg/myrepoe/manifests/prod, status 500" 
```
so this is an openshift issue, please report your findings over there. Might make sense to behave like the docker hub indeed.

```
time="2017-08-28T14:33:13+02:00" level=debug msg="Error body {\"errors\":[{\"code\":\"UNKNOWN\",\"message\":\"unknown error\",\"detail\":{\"ErrStatus\":{\"kind\":\"Status\",\"apiVersion\":\"v1\",\"metadata\":{},\"status\":\"Failure\",\"message\":\"imagestreams \\\"myrepoe\\\" not found\",\"reason\":\"NotFound\",\"details\":{\"name\":\"myrepoe\",\"kind\":\"imagestreams\"},\"code\":404}}}]}\n" 
```

Still, containers/image is throwing away this useful information. We should be able to parse this and report the “image stream not found” message to the users.
Yeah

Also @runcom it's not the server that is handling the upload differently but the client, in fact skopeo, here
https://github.com/projectatomic/skopeo/blob/0224d8cd387b69f1d48d7727e303e4e95a02c2af/cmd/skopeo/layers.go#L97-L106
is uploading all the blobs and then the manifest.

PutManifest must always come last, it's the registry API
Ok so would be an option to check if the manifest can-be-put before than writing?
Anyway the best place to discuss about this is that repo atm

> Ok so would be an option to check if the manifest can-be-put before than writing?

Nothing of that kind exists, AFAICT.

Given the design of the registry API, the only way to prevent the user from uploading all the layers and only later noticing that the repo is inaccessible for writing is for the _server_ to reject already the initial blob uploads (the blob uploads also include the repo name).

In the actual `docker/cli` this is implemented by doing a preventive `POST` on the resource and if that returns `202 Accepted` the layers are pushed.

Also, given that 202, the semantic is pretty clear.

Maybe here we can do the same?

> In the actual `docker/cli` this is implemented by doing a preventive `POST` on the resource

Could you elaborate, please? What resource, what request?

AFAICS from https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/master/distribution/push_v2.go#L170 , `docker/cli` also finishes upload of all the blobs before dealing with manifests at all.

Or do you mean the `POST /v2/…/blobs/uploads/`? Sure, that’s a mandatory part of the blob upload process, and containers/image does that as well, but the point is that the remote repo must actually reject the upload attempt at that time instead of accepting it and only checking permissions at manifest upload time.

(Well, strictly speaking, this is kind of theoretical, because all the attached logs show that the client has detected that the blobs already exists, so it does not attempt to upload any of them; so, the server has _no opportunity_ to say ”you don’t have write access to this repo” before we start to upload the manifest. I’m assuming that you saw the upload be actually performed, and succeed, sometime in the past.  Or maybe that’s not the case and we are only talking about the ”skipping fetch of repeat blob” process?)

@mtrmac sorry for being unclear.

If I understand the flow correctly, in the `docker/cli` things are happening in this order:

- The users initiate the upload via the 'docker image push <something>` command
- After some magic, layer are being pushed in a loop
- While pushing the [`blobs.Create`](https://github.com/docker/cli/blob/master/vendor/github.com/docker/distribution/registry/client/repository.go#L704) is called twice for each layer, [here](https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/9b4a616e4b3819ef062780b5fd854616be2c22bb/distribution/push_v2.go#L336) and [here](https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/master/distribution/push_v2.go#L390) - the first call is supposed to do the actual creation but if [it doesn't and does not trigger errors](https://github.com/docker/cli/blob/master/vendor/github.com/docker/distribution/registry/client/repository.go#L738) then it does not fill  the [`layerUpload`](https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/master/distribution/push_v2.go#L389) variable causing a retry. After all that happening the blob is actually [uploaded](https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/master/distribution/push_v2.go#L398)

So AFAICS the current upload rejection is implemented client side which is not ideal but at least this is managed in some way.

I think we have two different options here:
- Reproduce client side rejection in `containers/image`
- Report this behavior to the current registry implementations we use so that it can be handled also at that level

In any case the user needs to be warned about the fact that she/he doesn't have access to the repo or that it doesn't exists.

This can sound stupid but I've seen various situations where I personally or others I work with we have lost time figuring out this. Also in setups where one has Origin (for example) but he is not the cluster admin can be very difficult to debug the problem.

Pretty sure by client, you mean the Docker daemon and not the docker/cli, the cli just calls the daemon which in turns calls the registry. We're interested in what happens in the daemon here.
BTW I'll take a look at this shortly.

The description in https://github.com/projectatomic/skopeo/issues/402#issuecomment-326888468 describes in detail in how _blobs_ get uploaded, but AFAICT not a “preventive POST on the resource and if that returns 202 Accepted the layers are pushed.” to determine whether “the manifest can-be-put before than writing”.

---

Potentially confusing is that these log entries
```
Skipping fetch of repeat blob sha256:b25889d273102779a1d372b4e6590da9916fe594300bf1573cb9a6521a57c25e
```

1. Do _not_ actually require write permission; we can detect that a blob re-upload is not necessary without any write permissions; i.e. no failure can happen for the ”Skipping fetch” operations if the remote repo is not writable; the manifest upload is really the first operation that can fail in this particular case.
2. _Only_ happen if the blobs are already present at the destination, which is not generally the case; usually uploading a new image means uploading at least one blob.

---

> The only thing to fix here is IMO bubbling up the error from Origin (/cc @mtrmac ...).

Seems so; well, that and the ”Skipping fetch of repeat blob“ log lines should probably be improved to say something like ”Blob $digest already exists at the destination”.
I think we have much better pulling/pushing semantics, Closing, reopen if this is still an issue.
